---
title: "Day 3: Python and conda"
subtitle: "Morning brief, Wednesday July 15"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-15
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "ProCSI C Training  |  Day 3"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Where this fits

- Yesterday you learned to drive the computer by typing. Today you get the language you will use every remaining day: **Python**.
- Using Forward Euler numerical integration, the Blender animation script, and PyChrono next week are all Python. 
    - This is a "foundation day" -- you will use Python every day from now on.
- **conda** is the sidekick: it installs Python and its packages for you, cleanly.

## Today's target: build the Draft-Day app {.smaller}

- **By 4:00, your team can:** run a Python app, from the command line in your conda environment, that runs our morning ritual:
    - reads `students.txt` (one first name per line),
    - picks **5 captains** at random, skipping anyone who was already captain this week,
    - then runs the **draft**: captains take turns (round-robin), and on each turn the captain **types the name of the free agent they pick**, until everyone has a team,
    - prints the five teams, and saves today's captains and teams to a history file, so tomorrow's run remembers who has been captain.
- Keep the draft honest: always show whose turn it is and who is still available, and reject a pick that is taken or misspelled.
- **The prize:** at 4:00 we vote, and the winning team's app becomes the camp's official Draft-Day app, used every morning for the rest of camp.

## How to start

- Install **Miniforge**: it gives you `conda`, which manages Python for you.
- Create your camp environment once, then activate it every day:
  - `conda create -n camp python numpy matplotlib`
  - `conda activate camp`

## No "starter material" today

- Build the app from scratch, with Claude as your guide.
- Warm up first: ask Claude "Teach me Python variables, loops, and functions with tiny examples I can run."
- Then build: tell Claude the drafting rules in plain English ("20 students, 5 captains, no repeat captains this week, captains take turns picking...") and ask it to help you build the app **step by step**, explaining each piece.
- Stuck on the turn-taking? Ask: "How do I loop over the captains in turns, read each pick with input(), and reject names that are taken or misspelled?"

## Side quest: git and GitHub {.smaller}

- Yesterday's `git clone` is all the git this camp needs. Get familiar/learn more about the following:
- **git** tracks versions of files on your machine; **GitHub** hosts shared copies in the cloud. Together they are how software teams work.
- There'are safe places to play and test your git/GitHub understanding:
  - **GitHub Skills** ([skills.github.com](https://skills.github.com)): official hands-on mini-courses in a practice repository (free account needed).
  - **Learn Git Branching** ([learngitbranching.js.org](https://learngitbranching.js.org)): a visual git sandbox in the browser, no account, nothing to install.
  - **first-contributions** ([github.com/firstcontributions/first-contributions](https://github.com/firstcontributions/first-contributions)): make your first real pull request in a beginner-friendly repository.

\

- NOTE: if you don't have a GitHub account, you should create one today. It'll outlast this camp.

## Your end-of-day talk

- ~10 minutes: run your Team Shuffler **live on the real roster**, and tell us one thing about Python that surprised you.
- **Start or Close with your team's quote of the day.**
- Stretch goal:
    - Write an app that remembers the captains and teams from the previous days of the week, and uses that history to shuffle the teams differently today.
    - We'll use the best application starting tomorrow.
